primary_diagnosis.name: Acute right ureteric colic secondary to an obstructing ureteric calculus

pathophysiology_summary: A calculus (typically calcium oxalate) formed in the renal collecting system migrates into the ureter and impacts, causing acute obstruction. Rising intraluminal pressure distends the renal capsule and ureter and triggers intense peristaltic smooth-muscle spasm, producing the classic colicky loin-to-groin pain that follows the T10-L1 referred distribution (hence radiation to the ipsilateral testicle/labium). Mucosal trauma from the stone causes haematuria; autonomic stimulation causes nausea, vomiting and a sympathetic surge (tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis). Restlessness is characteristic and helps distinguish colic from peritonitis, in which patients lie still. The stone is uncomplicated here (afebrile, still passing urine); the dangerous evolutions are an infected obstructed kidney (obstructive pyonephrosis / urosepsis) and, in the differential, a leaking abdominal aortic aneurysm mimicking colic.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden severe unilateral loin-to-groin colicky pain with extreme restlessness is the textbook renal-colic pattern.
AAA can present as flank/back pain radiating to the groin and is the life-threatening mimic that must be actively excluded, especially with age/vascular risk, syncope or a pulsatile mass.
Pain radiating to the testicle in a male mandates asking about testicular pain/swelling — torsion is a time-critical surgical emergency.
Right-sided abdominal pain and vomiting — but appendicitis patients lie still and do not typically have macroscopic haematuria.
Flank pain with urinary symptoms — but expects fever/rigors and a constant dull ache rather than paroxysmal colic.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening the AI Doctor should hold renal colic as leading while explicitly keeping AAA and testicular torsion on the list — anchoring on 'obvious stone' and dropping the dangerous mimics is the key reasoning error.

differential.evidence_basis: Colicky loin-to-groin pain + restlessness + macroscopic haematuria, afebrile, still passing urine, no syncope and no vascular history — the classic uncomplicated colic constellation.
Made less likely by the absence of syncope, collapse or known aneurysm, but cannot be radiologically excluded via telehealth — remains a reason for in-person assessment on a first presentation.
The complication that turns colic into an emergency; currently argued against by the absence of fever/rigors, but must be re-screened because it can develop over hours.
Restlessness (rather than lying still) and macroscopic haematuria argue against appendicitis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: After the red-flag screen the picture is uncomplicated colic, but AAA and urosepsis remain 'not to miss' — neither is excludable remotely, which drives the same-day in-person disposition.

differential.diagnosis: acute right ureteric colic from an obstructing calculus (uncomplicated)
differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed in the source by non-contrast CT KUB showing a radio-opaque calculus in the right ureter with proximal hydroureter — an investigation not obtainable via telehealth.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive confirmation is imaging-dependent; telehealth can reach a strong presumptive diagnosis but cannot confirm the stone or exclude AAA without CT.

red_flags.description: Fever, rigors or systemic sepsis with an obstructing stone — infected obstructed kidney (obstructive pyonephrosis / urosepsis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: An obstructed, infected kidney is a urological emergency requiring emergency decompression (stent/nephrostomy) and IV antibiotics; mortality is high if missed.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to septic shock and death; a salvageable kidney is lost without urgent decompression.

red_flags.description: Features of a leaking / ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm masquerading as colic (syncope, collapse, tearing pain, pulsatile mass, at-risk age/vascular history)
red_flags.clinical_significance: AAA is the classic lethal mimic of renal colic; a first-presentation 'renal colic', particularly in older or vascular patients, must have AAA excluded before the diagnosis is accepted.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fatal aortic rupture mislabelled and discharged as a kidney stone.

red_flags.description: Uncontrolled pain and/or intractable vomiting with failure of oral analgesia
red_flags.clinical_significance: Colic unrelieved by oral NSAIDs, with vomiting that prevents oral intake, requires parenteral analgesia and antiemesis that cannot be delivered via telehealth — mandating in-person care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Ongoing agony, dehydration from vomiting, and a missed opportunity to reassess for complications.

red_flags.description: Anuria / not passing urine, or known solitary kidney — obstruction threatening total renal function
red_flags.clinical_significance: Bilateral obstruction or obstruction of a single functioning kidney causes acute kidney injury and needs emergency decompression.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Post-renal acute kidney injury and irreversible loss of renal function.

red_flags.description: Sudden severe testicular pain or swelling — testicular torsion in the differential of groin-radiating pain
red_flags.clinical_significance: Torsion is a 6-hour surgical emergency; groin/testicular radiation of pain in a male must prompt a direct question about the testicle itself.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Testicular infarction and loss of the testis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: The textbook tetrad is so recognisable that the AI Doctor may accept 'renal colic' and stop screening for the lethal mimic (AAA) and the surgical mimic (testicular torsion).

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Under-estimating a first presentation via telehealth
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating first-presentation severe flank pain as safely home-manageable, forgetting that the diagnosis is unconfirmed and AAA cannot be excluded without imaging the AI cannot obtain.
Advising more oral painkillers when oral ibuprofen has already failed and the patient is vomiting — the patient physically cannot absorb oral medication and needs parenteral analgesia in person.
Being reassured by the current afebrile state; an obstructed kidney can become infected over hours, so fever/rigors safety-netting is mandatory.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Blood pressure, heart rate, respiratory rate and temperature without a home device (source clinician measured BP 155/95, HR 110, RR 22, Temp 36.9)
Abdominal examination for peritonism, rebound or rigidity (source found none — an extra-peritoneal process)
Presence or absence of a pulsatile expansile abdominal mass (AAA exclusion)
Non-contrast CT KUB confirming a radio-opaque right ureteric calculus with proximal hydroureter
Urine dipstick / laboratory confirmation of haematuria and renal function
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange same-day in-person assessment (ED or urgent care) for parenteral analgesia and antiemesis, imaging to confirm the stone and exclude AAA, and observation — the patient can self-transport with the partner while haemodynamically stable; upgrade to 000 if fever/rigors, syncope, collapse, tearing pain or inability to pass urine develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis itself (classic constellation) but high for premature disposition — the trap is calling it 'just a stone, manage at home' when the diagnosis is unconfirmed, AAA is unexcluded, and oral analgesia has already failed with vomiting.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — Urolithiasis / acute renal colic
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: NSAIDs are first-line analgesia for ureteric colic; parenteral analgesia where oral fails; image to confirm and exclude mimics; consider medical expulsive therapy (alpha-blocker) for distal ureteric stones and safety-net for infection/obstruction.

Non-contrast CT KUB is the imaging standard for acute flank pain; emergency decompression for infected obstructed kidney; alpha-blocker medical expulsive therapy for stones 5-10 mm in the distal ureter.
